name: Josephine Cochrane's Dishwasher
short_description: The first patented mechanical dishwashing machine, using water-jet pressure and wire compartments to automate the washing of dishes — the origin of purpose-built domestic cleaning appliances.
long_description: Patented by Josephine Cochrane in 1886 (US Patent 355,139) and demonstrated to wide acclaim at the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago, this machine automated a household chore that had always been done by hand. Wire compartments sized for plates, cups, and saucers were mounted in a flat wheel inside a copper boiler; a motor rotated the wheel while hot soapy water was pumped up from the bottom and rained down on the dishes — the first design to clean by water pressure rather than mechanical scrubbers. Commercial establishments (hotels, restaurants) adopted it first because early models required hot-water infrastructure and cost too much for most households. Cochrane founded the Garis-Cochrane Manufacturing Company to produce it; the company was eventually acquired by Hobart Manufacturing and the product line later became part of KitchenAid. As the origin point on the domestic-labor spectrum, it shows how a fixed, single-task machine can replace a repetitive physical chore entirely — the design logic that still governs purpose-built home appliances today.
ai_note: Purely mechanical: a motor-driven rotating rack inside a copper boiler, with a pump that jets hot water. No computation, sensing, or machine learning of any kind.

images.caption: Patent drawing for Josephine Cochrane's dishwashing machine (US Patent 1,223,380, 1917), showing the rotating wire-rack wheel, copper boiler, and water-jet mechanism that descends from her original 1886 design.
images.alt_text: A black-and-white technical patent drawing of an early mechanical dishwashing machine, showing a cylindrical boiler with a rotating rack wheel inside and water-pump plumbing labeled with figure numbers.

sources.title: The Woman Who Invented the Dishwasher
sources.access_notes: Overview of Cochrane's invention, 1886 patent, World's Columbian Exposition win, company founding, and use of water pressure over scrubbers.

sources.title: Josephine Cochrane
sources.access_notes: Patent No. 355,139 issued December 28, 1886; mechanical details (wire compartments, copper boiler, rotating wheel, water-jet pump); company history; acquisition by Hobart and eventual KitchenAid lineage.

sources.title: Dishwasher — History
sources.access_notes: Contextualizes Cochrane's 1886 machine as the first to use water pressure rather than scrubbers; notes George Butters as co-builder; 1893 Columbian Exposition debut.

interactions.title: Load the racks and let water do the scrubbing
interactions.description: Stack plates, cups, and saucers into the wire compartments sized to hold each shape in place, then run the machine so jets of hot soapy water rain over them — cleaning by water pressure instead of hand-scrubbing, which had chipped Cochrane's heirloom china.

interactions.title: Turn the crank to run a wash
interactions.description: An operator drove the flat rack-wheel inside the copper boiler so the dishes rotated through the rising water — the machine could be worked by a hand crank, a step up from earlier designs that required pouring boiling water over dishes by hand.

interactions.title: Clear a hotel kitchen's dishes in minutes
interactions.description: In commercial models, kitchen staff could wash and dry a large load of dishes in a single short cycle, cutting the labor of hand-washing for a busy hotel or restaurant — Cochrane sold to institutions because the machine cost too much for most households.

interactions.title: Pitch it across a hotel lobby
interactions.description: Cochrane personally walked into the Palmer House in Chicago in 1887 to demonstrate and sell the machine, landing her first order — she later recalled crossing a hotel lobby alone to make the sale as one of the hardest things she ever did.

interactions.title: Run nine of them at the World's Fair
interactions.description: Nine Garis-Cochran machines were installed and operated in the restaurants and pavilions of the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago, where the design won the top award for mechanical construction and durability and drew orders from across the country.

interactions.title: Outfit a hospital or college kitchen for sanitation
interactions.description: Beyond hotels and restaurants, hospitals and colleges adopted the machines because the hot-water wash met strict sanitation requirements that hand-washing struggled to guarantee.

interactions.title: Swap in racks shaped for specialty service ware
interactions.description: Because the machine cleans by holding each piece in a fitted wire compartment and raining water over it, a kitchen could commission compartments shaped for unusual items — tall stemware or oversized platters — to wash service ware that doesn't fit standard racks.

interactions.title: A mobile demonstration unit for trade fairs
interactions.description: Given that Cochrane sold by showing the machine running in front of buyers, a self-contained demo model with its own water tank and hand crank could be wheeled from fair to fair to wash a load in front of a crowd without needing plumbed-in hot water.
